steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the author of Text 2 would most likely say about the description of the first showing of Arrival of the Train given in Text 1. Text 2 argues that there is no contemporary evidence audiences panicked, yet the story persists because “our belief that the coming of film was transformative is so strong that we invented and keep retelling a founding myth.” The author of Text 2 thinks the story is driven by belief in film’s significance, not by facts.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It is not based on evidence and reflects film historians' belief that they are more sophisticated than today's audiences.’: Text 2 says historians perpetuate the myth, not that they see themselves as superior to today’s audiences. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘It expresses a view about the transformative nature of film that film historians tend to regard as naïve.’: Text 2 says film historians repeat the myth; they do not dismiss the transformative view as naïve. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘It is more motivated by a perception of the significance of the invention of film than by facts.’: This directly reflects Text 2’s argument — the myth is sustained by belief in film’s transformative significance, not by evidence. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘It reflects a misconception that is widely believed because it captures early film audiences' sense that the new medium was alarming.’: Text 2 says there is no evidence early audiences were alarmed; the story is invented, not grounded in their actual reactions. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
